channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Dead as Disco Preview - Dead Men Drop No Combos
item.content:encoded:
Rhythm games are having a renaissance. Games like Rift of the NecroDancer have riffed on the classic track-based gameplay, and other games, like Hi-Fi Rush, are fusing rhythm with other genres to create something new.

Dead as Disco falls into the same category as Hi-Fi Rush. Fusing rhythm game timing and incentives with the action combat popularized by the Batman: Arkham franchise, Dead As Disco is something entirely its own.

Its combat isn’t really as expressive as other action games, but the setpieces of the main game and incredible customization features make it worth seeking out for any rhythm heads, even in Early Access.

Arkham’s Final Form

If you know how Arkham combat works, you know how Dead as Disco works. One button does your strikes, while the other buttons are for countering and dodging. It’s simple but effective, and the simplicity is much more fun to execute in the rhythm game context.

One smart new addition is the Fever Rush. As you fill a meter, you can hold a trigger and an attack button at the same time to unleash a flurry of blows in time to the music. It is a great way to punish attacks with a long cooldown, and it certainly helps that it makes you look like a timing god.

Charlie Disco (yes, that’s his real name)’s moveset expands as you play more levels, earn more fans, and redeem those fans for new skills. These include system level upgrades (like extra health) or brand-new techniques (like a ranged attack in the form of a thrown drumstick).

The structure is Mega Man-esque in its current form. You start on a stage select screen where you can choose from various bosses to fight, each a former band member who sold out Charlie for fame.

Levels are similar structurally: around 7-minute slugfests that start with you taking out hordes of enemies, teaching you mechanics, and culminating in a boss fight that tests what you learn. Every stage is a spectacle, with varied setpieces themed to each stage’s music that make them gorgeous to look at.

There are 4 of these fights in Early Access. It may not sound like much, but luckily, high score chasing is fun, and the mode Infinite Disco has one of the best custom track systems I’ve ever seen.

A Customization Sensation

Custom tracks are a massive part of any rhythm game. Truly, it’s not really a rhythm game until you can play Megalovania in it. How Dead as Disco innovates on this idea is the real selling point of the game.

Do you have a .mp3 file of a song you like on your computer? You can play it in Dead as Disco. You can upload any song, type in the BPM (I used this tool to find them online), and you have a playable song.

I used a lot of video game soundtracks that I had on my computer to test it. Sea of Stars’s “Battle On!” and Persona 4 Golden’s “Reach Out to the Truth” both worked quite well. Slower songs, as you might imagine, aren’t very fun.

These uploaded songs default to a survival mode, where you try to survive for the length of the song and get as high of a score as you can. However, if you want, you can also use the advanced editor to make some more complicated arenas with your own tracks. I’ll be leaving that part to some smarter people than me.

Dead As Disco | Final Thoughts

Overall, even though it’s in Early Access, Dead as Disco feels like it has plenty of worthwhile content and customization potential. The levels in the game are really fun arenas culminating in bombastic boss fights. While I wasn’t finding myself very invested in the narrative, the voice acting was good enough for me to sit through cutscenes between each stage.

Not to mention, like any rhythm game worth its salt, the customization is excellent. Not only am I sure that community members will make great stages, but anyone can upload their favorite songs to fight in an arena. 

If I got told that this was all I’m getting from Dead as Disco, I’d be satisfied. Because I know I’m getting more, I’m overjoyed.

Dead as Disco was previewed on PC with a code provided by the publisher over 3 hours of gameplay - all images were taken during the preview process.

Parker is a freelance writer based in Iowa, and the co-founder and editor of gaming satire site Palette Swap. Before Palette Swap, he was an editor at Hard… More about Parker



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/dead-as-disco-preview-dead-men-drop-no-combos
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
